title: Tariff Shock, Nvidia's $7B Bet, and the Memory Supercycle
subtitle: Liberation Day hits the data centre supply chain — chips exempt, everything else isn't.
summary: Episode Four examines how Trump's Liberation Day tariffs created a supply chain shock for digital infrastructure — exempting bare chips for data centre use but leaving servers, cooling equipment, power systems, and steel exposed to 10–45% levies. We analyse Nvidia's $7 billion strategic equity deployment across Intel, Marvell, and optical companies as it positions itself as architect of the AI supply chain; SemiAnalysis findings that memory now consumes 30% of hyperscaler capex (up from 8% in 2023); Related Digital's record $16 billion Stargate project financing for the 1 GW Michigan campus; and Digital Edge's $665 million green loan — Indonesia's largest-ever data centre project financing.
takeaways: Liberation Day tariffs exempt bare semiconductor chips for data centre use — but servers, cooling, networking, power, steel, and aluminium are all exposed to 10–45% levies
Hardware procurement costs for AI startups rose 10–20% in Q1 2026; analysts project 5–15% increase in overall data centre build costs
Nvidia deployed ~$7B in strategic equity: $5B Intel, $2B Marvell, $2B Lumentum, $2B Coherent, $2B Nebius — NVDA stock surged 6% on disclosure
SemiAnalysis: memory will consume 30% of hyperscaler data centre capex in 2026, up from 8% in 2023 — Nvidia receives preferential HBM pricing below market rates
Current global DRAM supply can only support ~15 GW of AI infrastructure deployment — a hard ceiling on pace of build-out
Related Digital finalising $16B Stargate financing: $2B Blackstone equity + $14B BofA bond issuance for 1 GW Saline Township, Michigan campus
Digital Edge secures $665M green loan — Indonesia's largest ever — for 500 MW phase 1 of 1 GW Bekasi campus; $4.5B total multi-phase investment
White House July 1 semiconductor review could expand or narrow the data centre chip carve-out — the regulatory hinge point for H2 2026 investment

chapters.title: Cold Open — The Carve-Out That Wasn't
chapters.description: Liberation Day's semiconductor exemption looked like good news for data centres — until the rest of the supply chain became exposed.

chapters.title: The Liberation Day Supply Chain Shock
chapters.description: Servers, cooling, networking, steel, and power systems are all subject to new tariffs, raising construction costs 5–15% and compressing lead times further.

chapters.title: Nvidia Becomes the Architect
chapters.description: Nvidia's $7B equity deployment — Intel, Marvell, optical companies, Nebius — signals a pivot from chip designer to full-stack AI infrastructure architect.

chapters.title: The Memory Supercycle
chapters.description: SemiAnalysis finds HBM will consume 30% of hyperscaler capex in 2026, up from 8% in 2023, with DRAM supply capping global AI deployment at ~15 GW.

chapters.title: Stargate's $16 Billion Record
chapters.description: Related Digital finalises a $2B Blackstone equity plus $14B bond structure for the 1 GW Oracle-OpenAI Michigan campus, the largest single data centre project financing ever.

chapters.title: Southeast Asia Raises Its Ambition
chapters.description: Digital Edge closes Indonesia's largest-ever data centre green loan — $665M for the first phase of a 1 GW campus in Bekasi — signalling that Indonesia's AI infrastructure decade has arrived.

chapters.title: What to Watch — Four Decisions Ahead
chapters.description: Stargate bond pricing, Q1 hyperscaler earnings and capex guidance, Nvidia memory supply disclosures, and the July 1 semiconductor tariff review.

chapters.title: Closing
chapters.description: Integration as both the engine of scale and the source of fragility — and the challenge of building supply chains that are both fast and resilient.

author: Digital Infrastructure Insider
